issues_found: Minor: Key point 'Mean probability of job loss in next 12 months increased to 14.4%' omits that this reading remains below the 12-month trailing average of 14.6%, slightly understating the nuance in the source

corrected_fields.summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York's March 2026 Survey of Consumer Expectations shows median one-year-ahead inflation expectations increased 0.4 percentage point to 3.4%, while three-year-ahead expectations rose to 3.1% and five-year-ahead remained unchanged at 3.0%. Gas price growth expectations surged to 9.4%, the highest level since March 2022. Labor market sentiment showed mixed signals with mean unemployment expectations reaching 43.5% (highest since April 2025), while mean job finding expectations improved to 45.9%.
corrected_fields.why_it_matters: Rising near-term inflation expectations, particularly the spike in gas price expectations to levels not seen since March 2022, could reinforce inflationary pressures and complicate Federal Reserve policy decisions. The deterioration in unemployment expectations (reaching the highest since April 2025) suggests households are increasingly concerned about economic conditions, though some improvement in job finding expectations indicates mixed labor market sentiment. The divergence between short-term (higher) and long-term (unchanged at 3.0%) inflation expectations provides insight into whether households view recent inflation as transient or persistent.
corrected_fields.macro_context: Short-term inflation expectations rose notably while medium-term also increased, suggesting households expect elevated prices to persist. The gas price spike to levels not seen since March 2022 is a key driver of near-term inflation expectations. Labor market concerns are rising as mean unemployment expectations reached the highest since April 2025, indicating households perceive increasing economic uncertainty, though job finding expectations showed some improvement.
